updates.summary: This is a feature-additive minor release with no breaking changes, introducing WebAuthn passkey support, a new WebStorage API, and several test runner enhancements. The browser engine updates (Chromium 149, Firefox 151, WebKit 26.5) are the most meaningful risk factor, as they could subtly affect existing visual or behavioral tests. Overall, the upgrade is low-risk and beneficial for teams needing passkey or localStorage testing support.
ramifications: Browser version bumps (Chromium 149, Firefox 151, WebKit 26.5) may cause minor rendering or behavioral differences in existing tests, especially screenshot or visual regression tests.
New video modes ('on-all-retries', 'retain-on-first-failure', 'retain-on-failure-and-retries') expand configuration options but are opt-in and do not affect existing configurations.
HAR and trace recordings now include WebSocket requests, which may increase artifact file sizes in projects that use WebSockets.
testInfo.errors now splits AggregateError sub-errors into separate entries, which could affect custom reporters or assertions that count or inspect error arrays.
Ubuntu 26.04 is now supported, which may be relevant for CI environments planning to upgrade OS images.
The new fullConfig.argv and fullConfig.failOnFlakyTests properties are purely additive and will not affect existing reporters unless they explicitly use these fields.

updates.summary: This is a major version upgrade from ESLint 9.0.0 to 10.5.0, spanning multiple minor and patch releases that include changes to rule violation reporting locations. Several rules (max-depth, max-nested-callbacks, max-lines-per-function, max-statements, no-with) now report errors at different AST node locations, which could affect tooling, error suppression comments, or CI output parsing. The changelog provided only covers the most recent changes and does not include the full set of breaking changes introduced throughout the 9.x to 10.x range.
breakingChanges: Major version bump from 9 to 10 implies potential breaking API changes not fully captured in this changelog excerpt
Rule violation locations changed: max-depth violations now reported on keywords instead of block statements
Rule violation locations changed: max-nested-callbacks violations now reported at function head
Rule violation locations changed: max-lines-per-function violations now reported at function head
Rule violation locations changed: max-statements violations now reported at function header
Rule violation locations changed: no-with violations now reported at the 'with' keyword
Stack tracking correction in max-nested-callbacks may change which violations are reported
Correction to max-depth handling for else-if chains may alter violation counts or locations
Node.js ICU support is now documented as a prerequisite, implying a possible runtime requirement change
Dependency @eslint/json updated to v2, which may introduce breaking changes in JSON linting behavior
ramifications: Existing eslint-disable comments tied to specific line/column locations may no longer suppress the correct violations due to shifted error locations
CI pipelines that parse ESLint output by line number or location may produce different results
Custom tooling or IDE integrations that rely on specific error locations for rules like max-depth or max-statements may behave unexpectedly
Projects using @eslint/json plugin may see behavioral changes due to the v2 upgrade
Test suites that assert specific ESLint error messages or locations will likely need updating
The full scope of breaking changes between 9.0.0 and 10.5.0 is unknown from this partial changelog and requires reviewing the complete migration guide
ICU support requirement for Node.js could affect environments running in stripped-down or minimal Node.js builds

advisories.summary: Withdrawn Advisory: eslint has a Stack Overflow when serializing objects with circular references

updates.summary: This is a major version upgrade spanning two major versions (15.x to 17.x), which typically implies breaking changes across multiple release boundaries. The changelog provided is minimal, only referencing a globals update with no detailed information about breaking changes or removed APIs. Given the sparse changelog and the two-major-version jump, careful review is warranted before upgrading.
breakingChanges: Two major version boundaries crossed (v15 to v17), each potentially containing breaking changes
Possible removal or renaming of global variable sets between major versions
Potential changes to the package's module format (CommonJS vs ESM) which occurred in the v14/v15 timeframe and may have continued evolving
API surface changes cannot be fully assessed due to incomplete changelog information
ramifications: ESLint configurations or other tooling referencing specific globals sets may break if those sets were renamed or removed
Any code importing specific named exports from the package may fail if the export structure changed
Downstream tools like eslint-plugin-* that depend on globals may need updates as well
Build or lint pipelines relying on specific global definitions could produce new errors or warnings
Testing environments that use globals for environment detection may behave differently

updates.summary: This is a significant major version jump spanning two major versions (9.x to 11.x), indicating substantial breaking changes and API modifications. Without detailed changelog information, it is difficult to assess the full scope of changes, but multi-major upgrades typically carry considerable risk. A thorough review of the migration guides between v9→v10 and v10→v11 is strongly recommended before upgrading.
breakingChanges: Multiple major version bumps (v9 to v11) likely include cumulative breaking API changes
Rule configurations and rule names may have been renamed, removed, or restructured
Plugin API and custom rule interfaces may have changed between major versions
Configuration file format (.htmlvalidate.json) may have breaking schema changes
Node.js minimum version requirements may have been increased
CLI flags and options may have been altered or removed
ramifications: Existing .htmlvalidate.json or .htmlvalidate.js configuration files may need to be updated
Custom plugins or rules written against v9 API may require rewriting
Validation results may differ due to new or changed rules, potentially surfacing new errors in previously passing HTML
CI/CD pipelines relying on specific exit codes or output formats may be affected
Integration with build tools (webpack, rollup, etc.) may require updated adapter packages
Test suites relying on specific validation output or rule error messages may break

updates.summary: This update patches CVE-2021-44906, a known security vulnerability, and bumps the follow-redirects dependency to address another security issue (CVE-2022-0536). The changes are primarily security-focused with no documented breaking changes, making this a strongly recommended upgrade. Staying on the current version leaves the application exposed to known exploitable vulnerabilities.
ramifications: Patching CVE-2021-44906 mitigates a prototype pollution vulnerability that could allow attackers to manipulate object prototypes.
Upgrading follow-redirects from 1.14.4 to 1.14.8 resolves CVE-2022-0536, which involves exposure of sensitive information via authorization headers during redirects.
No functional or API changes are expected, so existing usage should remain unaffected.
Skipping this update poses a security risk in environments where http-server is exposed to untrusted input or network traffic.

updates.summary: This is a minor version upgrade from 9.0.0 to 9.1.7 for the husky git hooks library. The changelog for the latest patch (9.1.7) shows only a minor fix to add a label to a deprecated warning message, indicating very low risk. The overall minor version bump likely includes several small improvements and fixes accumulated across 9.1.x releases.
ramifications: Deprecated warning messages will now include a 'husky' label for better clarity, improving developer experience when deprecated features are used.
Upgrading from 9.0.0 to 9.1.7 spans multiple minor/patch releases, so additional changes between 9.0.0 and 9.1.6 are not reflected in this changelog snippet.
No functional changes to git hook behavior are expected based on the provided changelog.

updates.summary: This is a major version jump spanning two major versions (15 to 17), which inherently carries risk even though the visible changelog snippet only shows patch-level bug fixes. The fixes address important correctness issues around merge conflict state preservation and stderr handling, which are meaningful improvements. However, the full scope of breaking changes across v16 and v17 is not reflected in the provided changelog, warranting careful review.
breakingChanges: Major version bump from 15 to 17 spans two major releases — undocumented breaking changes from v16 and v17 may exist beyond the provided changelog.

Switch from nano-spawn/execa to tinyexec (introduced in 16.3.0) changed stdout/stderr interleaving behavior, which was partially reverted in this patch — custom integrations relying on output parsing may be affected.
ramifications: Git merge conflict state (.git/MERGE_HEAD) was previously discarded when using --hide-unstaged or --hide-all; this is now fixed, which may change behavior in CI pipelines that run lint-staged during merges.
The tinyexec migration in 16.x may have introduced subtle differences in process spawning or environment variable handling compared to execa.
Any custom lint-staged configurations relying on previous stderr/stdout output behavior should be validated after upgrade.
Node.js version requirements may have changed across major versions — verify compatibility with your runtime environment.

updates.summary: This upgrade spans a significant range from 3.0.0 to 3.8.4, covering many minor releases that may include formatting behavior changes beyond the single changelog entry shown. The visible change is a bug fix for Markdown/MDX blank line handling in lists, which is low risk in isolation but the large version gap warrants review. Formatting changes across many versions could cause widespread diff noise in codebases with heavy Markdown or other file types.
ramifications: Formatting output may change across multiple file types due to the large version gap (3.0.0 to 3.8.4), potentially producing large diffs when re-formatting existing code.
Markdown and MDX files with nested list items may be reformatted differently due to the blank line bug fix, affecting existing files.
CI formatting checks (e.g., prettier --check) may fail on previously formatted files if formatting rules have subtly changed across versions.
Team workflows or pre-commit hooks that rely on exact formatting output may be affected and require a re-format pass across the codebase.
Only a partial changelog is shown; many intermediate releases between 3.0.0 and 3.8.4 are not represented, increasing uncertainty about all changes.

updates.summary: This is a major version upgrade from 16.0.0 to 17.13.0, spanning a significant version gap that likely includes breaking changes beyond the bug fixes described in this changelog snippet. The provided changelog only details 3 bug fixes for false positives/negatives in specific rules, but a major version bump typically signals API or configuration breaking changes. It is advisable to review the full changelog for all versions between 16.0.0 and 17.13.0 before upgrading.
breakingChanges: Major version bump from 16 to 17 may include breaking configuration changes not captured in this partial changelog
Potential changes to rule behavior or options that could affect existing lint configurations
Possible Node.js version requirement changes
Potential removal or renaming of deprecated rules or options
ramifications: Bug fix for `declaration-block-no-duplicate-properties` may surface new lint errors in codebases that previously had false negatives with `ignore: ['consecutive-duplicates(-*)']`
Fix for `selector-max-type` false positives for nested selectors may change linting results in projects using nested CSS
Fix for `selector-type-no-unknown` false positives for `install` selector reduces noise but may require config updates
CI/CD pipelines running stylelint may produce different pass/fail results after upgrade
Custom plugins or shareable configs may not be compatible with v17 without updates

advisories.summary: Stylelint has vulnerability in semver dependency

updates.summary: This is a major version upgrade spanning 4 major versions with several breaking changes including a switch to ESM module format, dropped Node.js support for versions below 20.19.0, and a requirement for stylelint 17+. The ESM conversion is particularly impactful as it can break CommonJS-based build tooling and configuration setups. Teams should carefully evaluate their Node.js version, stylelint version, and module system compatibility before upgrading.
breakingChanges: Module type changed to ESM — CommonJS require() will no longer work without adaptation
Dropped support for stylelint versions below 17.0.0 as a peer dependency
Dropped support for Node.js versions below 20.19.0
Updated to stylelint-config-recommended@18.0.0 which may introduce new or changed lint rules
ramifications: Projects using CommonJS (require()) to import this config will break and need migration to ESM or dynamic imports
CI/CD pipelines or environments running Node.js < 20.19.0 will need to upgrade their Node.js version
Projects on stylelint < 17.0.0 must upgrade stylelint first, which may itself introduce breaking changes
New or modified lint rules from stylelint-config-recommended@18.0.0 may cause previously passing stylesheets to fail linting
Configuration files like .stylelintrc.js using CommonJS exports may need to be converted to .stylelintrc.mjs or use ESM syntax
Tooling that wraps or extends this config (e.g., custom shared configs) may also break due to ESM incompatibility

updates.summary: This is a major version jump from 6.0.0 to 8.1.1, spanning two major versions, which typically implies potential breaking changes even though the provided changelog only documents a bug fix for non-standard CSS false positives. The changelog provided is minimal and may not represent the full set of changes across two major versions, making it difficult to fully assess risk. Teams using SCSS or other non-standard CSS in style tags may actually benefit from this fix, but the missing changelog details for the intermediate major version(s) warrant careful review.
breakingChanges: Two major version increments (6.x to 8.x) suggest potential breaking API or configuration changes not documented in the provided changelog
Possible changes to rule behavior, option signatures, or supported stylelint peer dependency versions between v6 and v8
Minimum Node.js or stylelint version requirements may have changed
ramifications: Existing stylelint-order rule configurations may behave differently or require updates
Peer dependency on stylelint may have stricter version requirements in v8
Projects using SCSS or other non-standard CSS in HTML style tags will see fewer false positives — a positive change
CI/CD linting pipelines should be tested to ensure no new or suppressed linting errors are introduced
Custom integrations or plugins depending on stylelint-order internals may break
